title: SkyStats
description: Hypixel SkyBlock stats tracker with 15,000+ MAU, built and maintained over three years. Rewrote the iOS client to SwiftUI and migrated the backend to MongoDB, reducing latency by 20%.

blog.subtitle: What fifteen thousand users taught me about building software

sections.heading: How it started
paragraphs: I built SkyStats at fifteen because I wanted a good way to check my Hypixel SkyBlock stats without opening a browser. One Discord post to a community server carried it to fifteen thousand monthly active users before I had any idea that was going to happen. That kind of unexpected scale is one of the faster ways to learn what makes software actually reliable.

sections.heading: Architecture that could grow
paragraphs: The feature set expanded quickly: inventory, ender chest, personal vault, collections, minions, skills, item drill-down, pet detail, equipment detail, bazaar charts, auction tracking. The only reason that was possible without the codebase becoming unmanageable was a sub-controller pattern where each list cell owned its own fetching, state, and refresh logic. The parent view didn't need to know about the data requirements of its children, which meant adding a new feature was mostly additive and rarely required changing existing code.

sections.heading: What I got wrong with charts
paragraphs: The bazaar charts were written from scratch using CoreGraphics instead of pulling in a charting library. That was the wrong call. Writing a horizontally zoomable candlestick view that handles touch input correctly cost about a week, and the result did exactly what five existing libraries already do. The lesson isn't that custom rendering is never worth it, it's that it needs a reason: performance constraints, a visual behavior that libraries can't express, or a tight size budget. None of those applied here.

sections.heading: Notification architecture
paragraphs: The auction notification feature was the single biggest driver of growth, moving daily actives past three thousand because no other Hypixel app was doing it at the time. The architecture had three pieces: a local cache of watched items stored on device, a server-side worker that compared live auction prices against each user's watchlist on a schedule, and background fetch on iOS to wake the app and deliver a local notification when a threshold was hit. Getting this right required understanding the constraints of iOS background execution, where the system controls how often your app is actually woken up and you have to do useful work in a narrow window.

sections.heading: The rewrite
paragraphs: Midway through the project I rewrote the iOS client from UIKit to SwiftUI and migrated the backend from Flask to a MongoDB-backed service at the same time. The visible change was a cleaner UI. The less visible change was that request latency dropped around twenty percent, because the new backend cached profile blobs and only recomputed networth when the cache was stale rather than on every request. Both changes shipped in the same update.

The MongoDB document store eventually hit the same wall as every document store used for relational data: evolving the schema required migrations that the document model made painful. SkyHub is the rebuild that started from a relational foundation.
